rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION VS. CORDWEE WONG ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 1. PLAINTIFF SAN FRANCISCO FEDERAL CREDIT UNION's HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION. Hearing required. The court's tentative appears below. For account ending 2728, held at Bank of America, the court denies the claim of exemption. Debtor Winnie Wong has not provided a financial statement as required by CCP 703.530. The supporting documentation she attaches does not support the claim of exemption. For the third party claims by Christina Wong to funds in account ending 7460 and by Cecilia Wong for the accounts ending 3563 and 9625, the court requires further documentation of these third-party claims. Christina Wong and Cecilia Wong are ordered to produce supporting documentation showing the source of funds in each of the accounts for which they claim ownership. The hearing will be continued to March 20, 2025, at 9 a.m. in Department 301. Supporting documentation must be served on the opposing party and filed with the court no later than 5 court days in advance of the hearing. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: VINCENT WILLIAMS VS. AUTO-CHLOR SYSTEM OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF VINCENT WILLIAMS' MOTION FOR SANCTIONS FOR OBSTRUCTION OF DISCOVERY AND VIOLATION OF DISCOVERY ORDER (ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 11 (Part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF VINCENT WILLIAMS' Motion For Sanctions For Obstruction Of Discovery And Violation Of Discovery Order. In any event, unless the parties have agreed to a particular format, "the responding party shall produce the information in the form or forms in which it is ordinarily maintained or in a form that is reasonably usable." (Id. section 2031.280(d).) This court interprets the April 17 order to incorporate (because it did not specify otherwise) the relevant law just recited that applies to the form of production of ESI. The court does not have before it in the present motion what the original RFPs said about the form of production or what Auto-Chlor specified in its responses about how documents would be produced. Lacking that information, and because the request to order a particular form of production was not raised in Williams's opening brief, the court does not order a particular form of production here, but it expects that Auto-Chlor will follow the requirements of the relevant statutes in making its production. If it fails to do so, Williams may seek further relief. Both parties seek sanctions. In light of the April 17 order's silence on the issues the court has decided or clarified with this motion, the court finds that both parties acted with substantial justification and sanctions are not warranted. The court reminds Williams to provide courtesy copies of moving papers for future filings in keeping with Local Rule 2.7(B). For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. rulings.Case Title: VINCENT WILLIAMS VS. AUTO-CHLOR SYSTEM OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions For Obstruction Of Discovery And Violation Of Discovery Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 11 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF VINCENT WILLIAMS' Motion For Sanctions For Obstruction Of Discovery And Violation Of Discovery Order. Plaintiff Vincent Williams moves for an order of sanctions against defendant Auto-Chlor System of Northern California Inc. for failing to timely comply with the court's order of April 17, 2024, which ordered Auto-Chlor to provide further responses to Special Interrogatory No. 7 and Request for Production Nos. 23-30. The court is further asked to order Auto-Chlor to provide its further responses to the discovery just specified in compliance with the April 17 order. For the reasons stated herein, the court grants the motion in part and denies it in part. As frequently occurs with discovery motions, the state of discovery described in the opening papers has changed. Williams's moving papers show through the Lofton Declaration that, at the time the opening brief was filed, Auto-Chlor had not yet complied and had stated, on February 4, 2025, Auto-Chlor needed two months to comply. (Lofton Dec. para. 10 & Ex. C.) Auto-Chlor opposed on the ground that it has since produced 7,500 documents and is in the process of producing more. Its moving papers substantiate through declaration only the factual claim that Auto-Chlor began producing documents on February 10, 2025 and do not substantiate the volume produced. (Horowitz Dec. para. 7.) Williams's reply papers agree that Auto-Chlor has begun production, but contend that Auto-Chlor has (1) produced documents as unsorted PDFs rather than in a reasonably usable format (Lofton Reply Dec. para. 3); and (2) has not yet produced amended responses to RFPs 23-30; wage statements or time records for "nearly half" of the PAGA employees; work schedules for all PAGA employees; and correspondence relating to meal and rest periods (Lofton Reply Dec. para. 4). Williams now further requests that the court order production of documents in the order and format as they are kept in the usual course of business. Finally, Auto-Chlor asks the court to strike the Williams reply because it raises issues about the format of production that were not raised in the motion. The court's April 17, 2024 order and the report and recommendation it adopted did not specify a deadline for production or amendment and did not specify the format of production. When a court discovery order is silent but the parties dispute what a court order requires, it makes sense to imply either reasonable terms or terms that are apparent from the governing discovery statutes. The court's April 17 order to Auto-Chlor to make further responses and produce responsive documents should be construed as meaning Auto-Chlor shall produce and amend within a reasonable time. Under the facts set out by both parties, including the completion of the meet-and-confer process required by the April 17 order, the court finds that a reasonable time to complete all obligations imposed by the April 17, 2024 order, including all production of documents, is within 30 days of notice of entry of this order. The court orders Auto-Chlor to do so. The April 17 order was silent about the format of production of electronically stored information. But the relevant statutes are not silent. The code provides that a propounding party "may specify the form or forms in which each type of electronically stored information is to be produced." (CCP section 2031.030(a)(2).) If the responding party "objects to a specified form for producing the information, or if no form is specified in the demand, the responding party shall state in its response the form in which it intends to produce each type of information." (Id. section 2031.280(c).) (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: LEVEL 5 DRYWALL, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. MUSIC CITY HOTEL LP, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Defendant Music City Hotel Lps Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, Request For Admissions, And Request For Production Of Documents, Set One; Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 10. 1 - PLAINTIFF LEVEL 5 DRYWALL, INC.'s Motion To Compel Defendant Music City Hotel Lps Responses To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, Request For Admissions, And Request For Production Of Documents, Set One. Moving party Level 5 Drywall, Inc., the plaintiff in this case, seeks an order compelling defendant Music City Hotel LLP to respond to Plaintiff's Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories, Request for Admissions, and Request for Production of Documents, Set One. The motion is unopposed. The moving papers establish that Plaintiff served this discovery on Music City Hotel and Music City Hotel hasnot responded. Under these circumstances, Music City Hotel has waived its objections to the requests and interrogatories. (CCP 2030.290(a); 2031.300(a).) The court orders Music City Hotel to provide code-compliant responses to the form interrogatories, special interrogatories, and request for production of documents within 20 days of notice of entry of this order. No objections may be asserted; they have been waived. The court does not order further responses to the RFAs. By companion order, the court has ordered that the matters asserted in the RFAs are deemed admitted. This is a full and complete response to the RFAs by operation of law. No additional remedy is prescribed by CCP 2033.280. The court shall impose monetary sanctions on a party who unsuccessfully opposes a motion to compel responses to discovery unless it finds that the party acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of sanctions unjust. (CCP 2030.290(d), 2031.300c.) The court does not find that Music City Hotel acted with substantial justification. Music City Hotel must pay $1066.75 to Level 5 Drywall Inc. in sanctions within 20 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. rulings.Case Title: LEVEL 5 DRYWALL, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. MUSIC CITY HOTEL LP, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Order Establishing Admissions; Request For Sanctions As To Defendant Music City Hotel Lp
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 10. 2 - PLAINTIFF LEVEL 5 DRYWALL, INC.'s Motion For Order Establishing Admissions; Request For Sanctions As To Defendant Music City Hotel LP. Plaintiff seeks an order deeming admitted the truth of matters specified in the requests for admission, set one, that Plaintiff served on defendant Music City Hotel, LLP. Plaintiff's moving papers, which are unopposed, establish that Plaitniff served RFAs on Music City Hotel and that Music City Hotel did not respond. Accordingly, Plaintiff is entitled to the order it seeks. The truth of matters specified in the RFAs is deemed admitted by Music City Hotel. (See CCP 2033.280.) Plaintiff also seeks sanctions. CCP 2023.280(c), provides that it is mandatory for the court to impose sanctions in this instance. The court finds Plaintiff's sanctions request reasonable and awards $716.75 in sanctions, payable by Music City Hotel to Plaintiff. The sanctions shall be paid within 15 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. rulings.Case Title: ALEXANDER XUE VS. HIGHFIELD HOSPITALITY INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to ANSWER to Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 3. PLAINTIFF ALEXANDER XUE's DEMURRER to ANSWER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Plaintiff's demurrer to the answer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend as to defenses 1 through 10. Defendant merely alleges terse conclusions of law-not ultimate facts-to support the defenses. (FPI Devel., Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 384 [defenses must be "averred as carefully and with as much detail as the facts which constitute the cause of action and are alleged in the complaint."]; Quantification Settlement Agreement Cases (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 758, 813 [same].) Plaintiff correctly notes that (1) defense 2 does not comply with CCP 458 [party pleading statute of limitations defense must cite code section]; and defense 11 (mistitled "Twelfth Affirmative Defense"), which reserves defendant's right to amend to plead unnamed defenses, is unnecessary. The demurrer to defense 11 is sustained without leave to amend For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 6 ( Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF JEFFREY UDELL's DEMURRER to ANSWER to COMPLAINT. Plaintiff's demurrer to defenses 1 and 3-35 is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Defendant merely alleges terse conclusions of law-not ultimate facts-to support the defenses. (FPI Devel., Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 384 [defenses must be "averred as carefully and with as much detail as the facts which constitute the cause of action and are alleged in the complaint."]; Quantification Settlement Agreement Cases (2011) 201 Cal.App.4th 758, 813 [same]; see also Weil & Brown, Cal. Prac. Guide Civ. Pro. Before Trial Ch. 7(I)-A, 7:35.1 ["A demurrer can be used to eliminate "boilerplate" affirmative defenses that often appear in answers (e.g., 'waiver,' 'estoppel,' 'unclean hands,' etc.)."] Plaintiff further argues that defendant's equitable defenses fail as a matter of law since this is a legal action. (See Abbott v. City of Los Angeles (1958) 50 Cal.2d. 438, 461 ["Plaintiffs are likewise correct in their contention that their second causes of action, for money judgments, constitute actions at law and therefore are not subject to the equitable defense of laches."]; FPI Devel., 231 Cal.App.3d at 384 ["For example, defendants allege as a conclusion that plaintiff's claim is barred by laches, an equitable defense that has no application to the plaintiff's legal claim."]. But the law is muddled regarding when equitable defenses can be raised to a legal claim. Cases have held that equitable defenses (namely unclean hands) can be asserted in a legal action. (Fiberboard Paper Products Corp. v. East Bay Union of Machinists, Local 1304, United Steelworkers of America, AFL-CIO (1964) 227 Cal.App.2d 675, 728 ["We are satisfied that the equitable defense of unclean hands is available in this state as a defense to a legal action."]; Unilogic, Inc. v. Burroughs Corp (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 612, 619-620; Padideh v. Moradi (2023) 89 Cal.App.5th 418, 437 ["We note that the unclean-hands doctrine is available as a defense in both equitable actions decided by a court and legal actions more often decided by a jury."]; see also Judicial Council of California v. Jacobs Facilities, Inc. (2015) 239 Cal.App.4th 882, 915 ["When equitable defenses are interposed to a legal cause of action, the 'proper rule' is for the court to hear and dispose of the equitable defenses first, before submitting the legal claim to a jury.") Other defenses such as laches are unavailable in legal actions. (Blaser v. State Teachers' Retirement System (2022) 86 Cal.App.5th 507, 540 ["Even where equitable claims are combined with actions at law, laches remains unavailable as a defense to the legal claims."].) This nuanced area of the law involves a claim by claim and defense by defense analysis. Plaintiff does not set out that analysis. In addition, since plaintiff seeks an injunction (equitable relief), the court is unwilling to dismiss all of the equitable defenses without leave to amend. (Complaint, 10:14-16.) (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JEFFREY UDELL VS. BHI RESIDENTIAL LONG TERM CORPORATION, A DELAWARE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF JEFFREY UDELL'S DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO COMPLAINT (ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery / Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, March 6, 2025, Line 6 ( Part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF JEFFREY UDELL's DEMURRER to ANSWER to COMPLAINT. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)
